result: The light this morning is the color of memory—soft at the edges, too bright at the center, like every summer I keep promising myself I’ll forget. I open the window anyway; the city rushes in with its usual perfume of diesel and yeast from the bakery below, and suddenly I’m nine again, barefoot on hot asphalt, trading stickers with the girl who moved away before I learned her last name. Time doesn’t pass; it leaks. It pools in the uneven sidewalk slabs outside the barbershop where my grandfather’s ghost still waits for a shave, hat in hand, telling the same joke about the Pole who walked into a bar with a duck on his head. I laugh every year, whether I want to or not. Between the fire escape and the opposite wall of brick someone has strung a clothesline of paper cranes; they revolve slowly in the updraft, origami prayers for a tenant who was evicted in April. I keep watering her geranium on the sill anyway, humming the hymn my mother claims she never taught me. There is no silence in this city, only layers of borrowed music: the tamale cart’s bell, the sirens that learned their minor key from mariachis, the click of my neighbor’s lighter at 2 a.m.—a sound as intimate as another heartbeat. I write all of it down, not to remember, but to prove I was here, that I once loved the world loudly enough to annoy the stars. The page dries. The cranes keep turning. The yeast keeps rising. I close the window; memory stays outside, knocking.

Time doesn’t pass; it leaks. There is no silence in this city, only layers of borrowed music: the tamale cart’s bell, the sirens that learned their minor key from mariachis, the click of my neighbor’s lighter at 2 a.m.—a sound as intimate as another heartbeat.
